---
title: "Surface registration: the sphere as a common frame"
---

The top rung of the [transform ladder](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder) does not move voxels - it moves
*vertices on a surface*. The cortex is a crumpled sheet, and two subjects' crumples never line up; we
cannot align them in the volume. The fix is to send each cortex to the *one shape they all share*: the
round sphere. The transform family is therefore the diffeomorphisms of the sphere, $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$,
with the rotation group $SO(3)$ as its rigid core - the group thread of the unit, now playing out on a
2-manifold. This chapter owns that machinery in `nitrix.geometry.sphere`:

- [`spherical_parameterize`](#sec-parameterize) - flatten a genus-0 cortical mesh onto $S^2$ by
  *Riemannian gradient descent* on $(S^2)^n$, minimising a conformal + areal *distortion energy*, with a
  *fold-safe line-search* that makes bijectivity an invariant of every step.
- [`surface_resample`](#sec-resample) - once two cortices live on the sphere, carry a field from one to
  the other by *barycentric resampling*; the default mode *conserves the area-weighted integral*, so
  label fractions and scalar means survive the transfer.
- [`spherical_conv`](#sec-spherical-conv) and `spherical_geodesic_distance` - Gaussian smoothing and
  great-circle distance on the sphere, built on the geometry unit's sparse-operator substrate.

The single new idea: a surface registration is *still* "minimise a mismatch energy over a parametrised
family of maps" - but the family is *maps of a sphere to itself*, and the regulariser that keeps the map
physical (its membership in $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$) is "*no triangle may fold*."

This chapter is written for a reader fluent in linear algebra who has read the geometry unit (meshes and
grids as discrete stand-ins for smooth manifolds) but has had no formal training in image registration,
graphics, or surface processing. The earlier chapters built the governing idea - *a registration is a map
between two geometries, found by minimising a mismatch energy over a parametrised family of transforms* -
and climbed the ladder from rigid through affine ([Transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-homogeneous)) to dense
diffeomorphisms ([Diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)). Every rung so far has lived in the *volume*:
the domain was a grid $\Omega_f$, the map was $\varphi:\Omega_f\to\Omega_m$, and the physicality
constraint was $\det J>0$ (no folding of the deformation field).

This chapter is the ladder's summit and its one genuine change of setting. The data is no longer an image
on a grid; it is a *scalar field on a folded 2-D sheet* - the cerebral cortex - embedded in 3-D. The same
four questions return - how is the map *represented*, *applied*, *scored*, and *found*, while staying
*physical* - but every answer is re-derived for the surface. We lean hard on the geometry unit, which
already owns the spherical embedding theory: we *build on it, not re-derive it*.

## A motivating problem {#sec-motivation}

You have T1 scans from two subjects. You run a surface pipeline and recover, for each, a triangle mesh of
the white-matter surface - the boundary between grey and white matter, a highly folded genus-0 sheet of
roughly 150,000 vertices - plus a scalar map on it, say cortical thickness or a task activation
$z$-statistic. You want to ask a group question: *is thickness greater in subject A than subject B at the
same patch of cortex?*

That question is ill-posed as stated, because subject A's vertex 90,210 is not subject B's vertex 90,210.
The two meshes have different vertex counts, different tessellations, and - fatally - different folding
patterns: A's central sulcus sits where B's precentral gyrus does. There is no volume transform $\varphi$
that lines up their cortices, because the cortex is a 2-D manifold crumpled into 3-D, and the crumpling is
individual. Aligning the *folds* in 3-D space is the wrong target; what we want to align is *position
along the cortical sheet*, intrinsic to the surface.

The strategy, which this chapter makes concrete, has two moves:

1. **Find a common frame.** Every genus-0 surface is topologically - and, by the uniformisation theorem,
   conformally - a sphere. So map each subject's cortex onto the unit sphere $S^2$, as undistorted as
   possible. The sphere is the *canonical common coordinate system* for the cortex: a point on it is "this
   much anterior, this much dorsal," independent of any one brain's folds. That is
   [`spherical_parameterize`](#sec-parameterize).
2. **Transfer fields across the frame.** With both cortices on the sphere (and, in a real pipeline,
   rotationally *registered* so anatomy aligns - see the closing note), resample subject A's thickness
   onto subject B's spherical mesh - or onto a shared template mesh like `fsaverage`. That is
   [`surface_resample`](#sec-resample). Now "the same patch of cortex" *means* "the same spherical
   coordinate," and the group question is well-posed.

`spherical_conv` and `spherical_geodesic_distance` are the supporting metric tools you need once you live
on the sphere - to smooth a noisy map respecting the surface, and to measure distance along it. We take
the three in turn, intuition to rigour.

## Why a sphere, and how to get there {#sec-parameterize}

### The sphere is the cortex's natural chart

Start from the topology. The cortex, taken as the white-matter or pial surface of one hemisphere with the
midline cut filled, is a *genus-0* closed surface - a sphere with no handles (the
[geometry foundations](../geometry/foundations.qmd#sec-discretization) page sets out the
mesh-as-manifold picture; the genus / Euler-characteristic machinery is owned there and in
[`signed_spherical_areas` / `is_bijective_sphere_map`](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding)). A genus-0
surface can be continuously deformed onto a sphere without cutting or gluing. Better than topology, the
*uniformisation theorem* promises a deformation that is *conformal* - angle-preserving - and essentially
unique. So the sphere is not an arbitrary choice; it is the cortex's natural global chart, the one surface
that every genus-0 cortex shares and onto which each maps almost-rigidly-in-spirit.

We want the vertex positions $\Phi=\{\phi_i\}\subset S^2$ such that the piecewise-linear map (original
vertex $v_i \mapsto$ spherical vertex $\phi_i$, *same faces*) is a *bijection* (every spherical triangle
keeps positive oriented area - no folds) and distorts the surface's geometry as little as possible.
Bijectivity is exactly the statement that the map is a genuine element of $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$ rather than
a self-overlapping fold; it is the surface counterpart of the volume rungs' $\det J>0$.
@fig-surface-parameterize shows the target: a bumpy genus-0 blob and its spherical image carrying the same
field.

```{python}
#| label: fig-surface-parameterize
#| fig-cap: "Spherical parameterisation. (a) a bumpy genus-0 surface with a scalar field; (b) its parameterisation onto $S^2$ — the **same faces**, the same field, every vertex now on the sphere; (c) the areal distortion the map necessarily incurs: where the surface bulged out (a gyrus) it is compressed onto the sphere (magenta, $a_{\\text{sphere}}<a_{\\text{cortex}}$), where it was tucked in (a sulcus) it is stretched (green). A conformal map keeps angles but cannot keep areas — controlling that trade-off is what the energy does."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def icosphere(subdiv=3):
    t = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    verts = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=float)
    verts /= np.linalg.norm(verts, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    faces = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ], dtype=int)
    for _ in range(subdiv):
        mid = {}; vlist = list(verts); new_faces = []
        def mp(i, j):
            key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
            if key in mid: return mid[key]
            m = (vlist[i] + vlist[j]) / 2.0; m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
            vlist.append(m); mid[key] = len(vlist) - 1; return mid[key]
        for (a, b, c) in faces:
            ab, bc, ca = mp(a, b), mp(b, c), mp(c, a)
            new_faces += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
        verts = np.array(vlist); faces = np.array(new_faces)
    return verts, faces

sph, faces = icosphere(3)

# A "cortex": deform the sphere radially by a few smooth bumps -> bumpy genus-0 blob.
centres = rng.normal(size=(6, 3)); centres /= np.linalg.norm(centres, axis=1, keepdims=True)
amp = rng.uniform(0.25, 0.55, size=6) * rng.choice([-1, 1], size=6)
def radial(v):
    r = np.ones(v.shape[0])
    for c, a in zip(centres, amp):
        r += a * np.exp(-3.0 * np.sum((v - c) ** 2, axis=1))
    return r
cortex = sph * radial(sph)[:, None]

field = np.sin(2.5 * sph[:, 0]) * np.cos(2.0 * sph[:, 1]) + 0.4 * sph[:, 2]

def face_area(V, F):
    v0, v1, v2 = V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 1]], V[F[:, 2]]
    return 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(v1 - v0, v2 - v0), axis=1)
distortion = np.log(face_area(sph, faces) / face_area(cortex, faces))

fcol = field[faces].mean(axis=1)
norm = plt.Normalize(field.min(), field.max()); cmap = plt.cm.coolwarm

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4.4))
def draw(ax, V, fc, title):
    pc = Poly3DCollection(V[faces], facecolors=cmap(norm(fc)),
                          edgecolors=(0, 0, 0, 0.08), linewidths=0.15)
    ax.add_collection3d(pc)
    lim = np.abs(V).max() * 0.85
    ax.set_xlim(-lim, lim); ax.set_ylim(-lim, lim); ax.set_zlim(-lim, lim)
    ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax.view_init(22, 35); ax.set_axis_off()
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11)

draw(fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 1, projection='3d'), cortex, fcol,
     "(a) bumpy genus-0 surface\nscalar field on the cortex")
draw(fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 2, projection='3d'), sph, fcol,
     "(b) spherical parameterisation\nsame field, same faces")
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, 3, projection='3d')
dn = plt.Normalize(-np.abs(distortion).max(), np.abs(distortion).max())
ax3.add_collection3d(Poly3DCollection(sph[faces], facecolors=plt.cm.PiYG(dn(distortion)),
                                      edgecolors=(0, 0, 0, 0.08), linewidths=0.15))
ax3.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax3.set_ylim(-1, 1); ax3.set_zlim(-1, 1)
ax3.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax3.view_init(22, 35); ax3.set_axis_off()
ax3.set_title("(c) areal distortion of the map\n$\\log(a_{sphere}/a_{cortex})$", fontsize=11)
fig.colorbar(plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=plt.cm.PiYG, norm=dn), ax=ax3,
             shrink=0.6, label="log area ratio")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Radial projection, and where it breaks

The obvious first attempt: push each vertex straight out from the surface's centroid onto the sphere -
*radial projection*, $\phi_i = r\,(v_i-c)/\lVert v_i-c\rVert$. This is exactly `init='radial'` in the
code. For a nearly-convex inflated blob it works and costs nothing. It breaks the moment the surface is
non-convex: a sulcal bank that folds back past the centroid projects *on top of* the gyrus next to it, and
two triangles land on the same patch of sphere with *opposite orientation* - a fold. A folded map is not a
bijection (not an element of $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$); vertex correspondence is destroyed silently.

So radial projection fails when the surface is folded - which a cortex emphatically is. We need two things
radial projection lacks: a *better starting point* that is already nearly fold-free, and an *iterative
refinement* that drives distortion down *while provably never folding*. `spherical_parameterize` supplies
both.

### A better start: the spectral embedding (owned by the geometry unit)

The default `init='spectral'` is the FastSurfer / recon-surf one-shot map, and it is *owned, derived, and
figured by the geometry unit* - see
[spectral embedding: a sphere in one eigensolve](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding). We do not
re-derive it here; we use its result. In one sentence: solve the Laplace–Beltrami generalised eigenproblem
$L\phi=\lambda M\phi$ on the mesh, take the *first three non-constant eigenfunctions* $(\phi_1,\phi_2,
\phi_3)$ - which on a round sphere *are* the coordinate fields $x,y,z$ - and normalise each vertex's triple
to the sphere. For an inflated cortex (already near-spherical), this lands almost on the sphere in a single
eigensolve, with no iteration. `spherical_parameterize` calls `spectral_sphere_embedding` to obtain
$\Phi^{(0)}$, reusing the cotangent operator it will need anyway (it builds `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` once
and threads it in via `laplacian=`).

The spectral map is fast but only *quality-gated* bijective: recon-surf tolerates a tiny flipped-area
fraction rather than proving zero folds. The fold *gate* itself -
[`is_bijective_sphere_map`](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding) (a degree-1 cover test: signed solid
angles sum to $\pm4\pi$ and no triangle flips against the dominant orientation) and the
[`signed_spherical_areas`](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding) it is built on - also live in the
geometry unit. We *build on them*: they are the fold-detector the refinement uses on every step. The other
inexpensive start, `init='radial'`, is there for near-convex inputs and falls through the same gate.

One detail `spherical_parameterize` fixes before refining: the spectral eigenfunctions carry an arbitrary
*sign gauge* (the eigenvector-sign symmetry of [the spectral chapter](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding)),
so the embedding may come out inside-out (all signed areas negative). The code measures the total signed
area and mirrors one axis if it is negative - `sign = jnp.where(jnp.sum(signed_spherical_areas(phi, faces))
< 0, -1, 1)`, then `phi = phi.at[:, 0].multiply(sign)` - so a bijective map always has positive
orientation. This is done with `jnp.where`, not a host branch, so it composes under `jit`/`grad`. With
`n_iterations=0` the function returns this gauge-fixed init alone - the recon-surf-grade one-shot map.

### The energy: conformal core, areal barrier, and the collapse trap

Now the refinement. We need a scalar *distortion energy* $E(\Phi)$ that is small when the map is faithful
and that we can differentiate. The right core is the *Dirichlet / conformal energy*, the surface analogue
of $\int\lVert\nabla\varphi\rVert^2$:

$$
E_{\text{conf}}(\Phi) \;=\; \tfrac12 \sum_{(i,j)} w_{ij}\,\lVert \phi_i - \phi_j \rVert^2
\;=\; \tfrac12\,\Phi^\top L\,\Phi,
$$ {#eq-conformal}

where $L$ is the *cotangent Laplacian* of the *original* mesh (the geometry unit's
[cotangent Laplace–Beltrami operator](../geometry/laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)) and $w_{ij}$ are its
cotangent edge weights. In the code this is exactly `0.5 * jnp.sum(p * apply_operator(lap, p))` - a
quadratic form applied through the sparse [apply seam](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-apply). Minimising
$E_{\text{conf}}$ over maps to the sphere yields the harmonic, angle-preserving map: the conformal core.

Here is the load-bearing subtlety. $E_{\text{conf}}$ has a *trivial global minimiser*: collapse *every*
vertex to a single point on the sphere. Then every $\phi_i-\phi_j=0$ and the energy is exactly zero. Pure
conformal descent will slide toward this useless "all of cortex maps to the north pole" solution. Avoiding
collapse is the whole problem. The cure has two parts, both present in the code.

**Part one: an areal term that explodes as any triangle shrinks.** Let $a_f(\Phi)$ be the signed spherical
area (`signed_spherical_areas`) of face $f$, and let the *target* area be the original face's share of the
total, scaled so the targets sum to $4\pi$: $\text{target}_f = 4\pi\,A^0_f/\sum_g A^0_g$ (in code,
`target = 4*pi*a0/sum(a0)` with `a0 = face_areas(mesh)`). Define the relative area $s_f =
a_f/\text{target}_f$ (so a perfectly area-true face has $s_f=1$). The energy uses the symmetric *ratio
barrier*

$$
E_{\text{area}}(\Phi) \;=\; \sum_f \Big( s_f + \tfrac{1}{s_f} - 2 \Big)
\;+\; 10^3 \sum_f \max\!\big(10^{-3} - s_f,\; 0\big),
$$ {#eq-area}

with $s_f$ floored at $10^{-3}$ inside the barrier for numerical safety. The first term $s+1/s-2$ is zero
at $s=1$, grows whether the face is too big *or* too small, and $\to\infty$ as $s\to0^+$ - so any global
shrink is infinitely penalised, killing the collapse minimiser. The second term is a *hinge* that activates
only on tiny or *negative* (flipped) areas; unlike a hard clamp it keeps a live *gradient* pushing a folded
triangle back toward positive area. The total energy the optimiser sees is

$$
E(\Phi) \;=\; \lambda_{\text{conf}}\,E_{\text{conf}}(\Phi)
\;+\; \lambda_{\text{area}}\,E_{\text{area}}(\Phi),
$$ {#eq-total}

with weights `conformal_weight` and `area_weight` (default $1$ each). The collapse-free conformal map is
the Choi–Lam–Lui (2015) insight - $E_{\text{conf}}$ minus an area reward has no collapse minimiser -
realised here through the explicit area barrier so the same machinery also serves the area-controlled
objective FreeSurfer's `mris_sphere` wants.

**Part two: centroid (Möbius) normalisation each step**, described next - it removes the gauge direction
along which collapse drifts.

### Finding the map: Riemannian descent with a fold-safe line-search

The vertices are *constrained* to the sphere: $\Phi\in(S^2)^n$, not free in $\mathbb R^{3n}$. Naively
stepping $\Phi - \alpha\nabla E$ walks off the sphere. The clean way to optimise on a constraint manifold
is *Riemannian gradient descent* - the same posture that put the optimiser on the Lie chart for the matrix
rungs, here on the product manifold $(S^2)^n$ - in three moves:

1. **Euclidean gradient.** $g=\nabla_\Phi E$ - and because the whole energy is pure JAX, this is just
   `jax.grad(_energy)`. No hand-derived gradient.
2. **Tangent projection.** At a point $\phi_i$ on a sphere of radius $r$, the only admissible motions are
   *tangent* to the sphere; the radial part of the gradient would push off it. Remove it:
   $$
   g^\perp_i \;=\; g_i \;-\; \frac{\langle g_i,\phi_i\rangle}{r^2}\,\phi_i.
   $$ {#eq-tangent}
   In code, `grad_t = g - jnp.sum(g * p, axis=-1, keepdims=True) / r2 * p`. The stiff fold barrier can make
   the raw gradient enormous at a near-flipped triangle, so the code then *normalises by the largest
   per-vertex tangent gradient* (`grad_t / (gmax + 1e-12)`), so the step size $\alpha$ bounds the largest
   vertex move and one bad triangle cannot make the whole step overshoot.
3. **Retraction.** A tangent step leaves the sphere slightly; pull it back by renormalising to radius $r$:
   $\phi_i \leftarrow r\,(\phi_i-\alpha g^\perp_i)/\lVert\cdot\rVert$. This is the `_retract` helper - and
   it is also where the *centroid (Möbius) normalisation* lives: after renormalising, subtract the mean
   (`q - jnp.mean(q, axis=0)`) and renormalise again. Spherical conformal maps are unique only up to a
   Möbius transform; recentring the cloud at the origin each step fixes the translational gauge and is the
   concrete mechanism - together with @eq-area - that blocks the collapse.

The physicality constraint - the analogue of $\det J>0$ from the volume rungs, here the condition that the
map stay in $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$ - is *no fold*: every $a_f>0$. We enforce it not by hoping the optimum is
fold-free but by making it an *invariant of the iteration*, via a *fold-safe backtracking line-search*.
Each outer step:

- record the current energy $e_0$ and current flip count $n_{\text{flip}}^0=\#\{f: a_f\le0\}$;
- propose the retracted candidate at step $\alpha$;
- *accept* it only if it both *lowers the energy* ($E(\text{cand})<e_0$) *and* does not increase the flip
  count ($n_{\text{flip}}\le n_{\text{flip}}^0$); otherwise *halve $\alpha$ and retry*, down to a floor
  $\alpha>10^{-5}$.

This is a `jax.lax.while_loop` inside the body, the outer loop a `jax.lax.fori_loop` over `n_iterations`.
The acceptance test is the heart of it: a step that would flip a triangle is rejected and the move shrunk,
so the iterate *can never leave the fold-free region*. Bijectivity is driven to strict (no flipped
triangles) and *held* there - exactly the "a diffeomorphism does not tear or fold" posture, transcribed to
the surface.

### Execution class and how to call it

`spherical_parameterize` is *host-orchestrated*, *not* a jittable kernel - the one place in this chapter
where you must not reach for `jax.jit`. It builds the cotangent operator host-side (NumPy assembly) once,
and for `init='spectral'` runs a host eigensolve; the per-iteration *body* (energy, tangent gradient,
fold-safe search) is pure JAX and runs on device, but the function *as a whole is neither jittable nor
differentiable w.r.t. `mesh.vertices`* (the operator weights are NumPy constants). Differentiating
*through* the parameterisation (e.g. a learned consumer needing $\partial\Phi/\partial V$) is out of scope;
the principled route would be implicit differentiation at the energy minimiser, a documented follow-up. The
contract from the [execution-class taxonomy](../geometry/foundations.qmd#sec-classes) holds: *never call a
host op inside a jitted loop* - call `spherical_parameterize` to *produce* a spherical map, then jit the
downstream numerics. Its full signature, with the defaults that matter:

```python
phi = spherical_parameterize(
    mesh,                       # inflated genus-0 Mesh; output keeps the same faces
    init='spectral',            # 'spectral' (recon-surf one-shot) | 'radial'
    n_iterations=200,           # 0 -> init only (the one-shot map)
    conformal_weight=1.0,       # lambda_conf in @eq-total
    area_weight=1.0,            # lambda_area in @eq-total
    step=0.05,                  # initial line-search step alpha
    radius=1.0,                 # output sphere radius
)                               # -> (n_vertices, 3) vertices on S^2
```

The return is vertex positions on the sphere; the faces are *unchanged*, so vertex correspondence to the
input is preserved exactly. That is what makes the next step - moving a field *between* spheres - possible.

## Transferring a field across spheres: barycentric resampling {#sec-resample}

Two cortices now live on the sphere, and - assuming a prior spherical *registration* has rotated/warped one
onto the other so anatomy aligns (the MSM/`sugar`/`josa` step of [Connecting outward](#sec-connections), a
literature pipeline stage that applies an element of $SO(3)$ or $\mathrm{Diff}(S^2)$ to one sphere, *not* a
`nitrix.geometry.sphere` symbol) - a point at a given spherical coordinate means the same anatomical
location in both. They still have *different tessellations* - different vertex counts and triangulations of
the same sphere. To compare subject A's thickness with subject B's at the same location, we must put both
on *one* mesh: resample A's field onto B's vertices (or both onto a shared template like `fsaverage`). This
is the `wb_command -metric-resample` analogue,
[`surface_resample`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py), and it
is the operation that makes cross-subject comparison concrete.

### Locate, then blend

For each *target* vertex, ask: which *source* triangle does it fall inside, and where inside it? Answer
with a single number per source vertex - the *barycentric weight* - and the target value is the weighted
blend of that triangle's three source values. A planar triangle with corners $v_a,v_b,v_c$ and an interior
point $p$ has a unique decomposition $p = w_a v_a + w_b v_b + w_c v_c$ with $w_a+w_b+w_c=1$ and each
$w\ge0$; geometrically each $w$ is the area of the sub-triangle *opposite* its corner, divided by the
whole. @fig-surface-bary shows both the global correspondence and a single located vertex with its weights.

```{python}
#| label: fig-surface-bary
#| fig-cap: "Barycentric resampling between two tessellations of the sphere (shown in the plane — locally the sphere search is the radial projection of the target onto a source triangle's plane, so the planar picture is the exact local model). (a) a coarse **source** mesh (blue) and a denser **target** point set (red); each target vertex is located in the source triangle containing it and linked to that triangle's three corners, the link opacity scaling with the barycentric weight. (b) one target $t$ in its source triangle: the three weights $w_a,w_b,w_c$ are the normalised areas of the sub-triangles opposite each corner and sum to 1, so a constant source field is reproduced exactly."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

src = np.array([
    [0.05, 0.10], [0.95, 0.05], [0.50, 0.95],
    [0.40, 0.42], [0.68, 0.50], [0.30, 0.70],
])
src_tris = np.array([
    [0, 3, 5], [0, 1, 3], [1, 4, 3], [1, 2, 4], [2, 5, 4], [3, 4, 5], [0, 5, 2],
])
tx, ty = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(0.12, 0.86, 5), np.linspace(0.16, 0.84, 5))
tgt = np.stack([tx.ravel(), ty.ravel()], axis=1) + rng.normal(scale=0.012, size=(25, 2))

def bary(p, a, b, c):
    v0, v1, v2 = b - a, c - a, p - a
    d00, d01, d11 = v0 @ v0, v0 @ v1, v1 @ v1
    d20, d21 = v2 @ v0, v2 @ v1
    det = d00 * d11 - d01 * d01
    wb = (d11 * d20 - d01 * d21) / det
    wc = (d00 * d21 - d01 * d20) / det
    return np.array([1 - wb - wc, wb, wc])

def locate(p):
    best, bw, bi = -np.inf, None, None
    for i, (ia, ib, ic) in enumerate(src_tris):
        w = bary(p, src[ia], src[ib], src[ic])
        if w.min() > best:
            best, bw, bi = w.min(), w, i
    return bi, bw

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))
ax = axes[0]
ax.triplot(src[:, 0], src[:, 1], src_tris, color="#1f77b4", lw=1.6, label="source mesh")
ax.plot(src[:, 0], src[:, 1], "o", color="#1f77b4", ms=6)
ax.plot(tgt[:, 0], tgt[:, 1], "s", color="#d62728", ms=5, label="target vertices")
for p in tgt:
    i, w = locate(p); ia, ib, ic = src_tris[i]
    for idx, wt in zip((ia, ib, ic), w):
        alpha = float(np.clip(0.15 + wt, 0.0, 0.9))
        ax.plot([p[0], src[idx, 0]], [p[1], src[idx, 1]], color=(0.4, 0.4, 0.4, alpha), lw=0.6)
ax.set_title("(a) each target vertex (red) is located in a source triangle;\n"
             "edges to the 3 source corners weighted by the barycentric weight")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")

ax = axes[1]
p = tgt[12]; i, w = locate(p); ia, ib, ic = src_tris[i]
A, B, C = src[ia], src[ib], src[ic]
ax.add_patch(plt.Polygon([A, B, C], facecolor="#cfe8ff", edgecolor="#1f77b4", lw=2))
ax.fill([p[0], B[0], C[0]], [p[1], B[1], C[1]], color="#ffd9d9", alpha=0.7, zorder=0)
ax.fill([A[0], p[0], C[0]], [A[1], p[1], C[1]], color="#d9ffd9", alpha=0.7, zorder=0)
ax.fill([A[0], B[0], p[0]], [A[1], B[1], p[1]], color="#fff3cc", alpha=0.7, zorder=0)
label_off = {0: (8, 8), 1: (8, 8), 2: (8, -24)}
for j, (V, name, wt) in enumerate(zip((A, B, C), ("$v_a$", "$v_b$", "$v_c$"), w)):
    ax.plot(*V, "o", color="#1f77b4", ms=10)
    ax.annotate(f"{name}\n$w={wt:.2f}$", V, textcoords="offset points", xytext=label_off[j], fontsize=11)
ax.plot(*p, "s", color="#d62728", ms=11)
ax.annotate("target $t$", p, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, -16), color="#d62728", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("(b) the three weights are the areas of the\nsub-triangles opposite each corner ($w_a+w_b+w_c=1$)")
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off"); ax.relim(); ax.autoscale_view()
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The spherical search, done robustly

On the sphere the "containing triangle" is found radially: a target unit vector $t$ falls in the source
triangle whose three great-circle edges all keep $t$ on their interior side. The code scores each face by
the *minimum signed great-circle-edge distance* - for edge $a\!\to\!b$, the sign of $\langle t, a\times
b\rangle$ - and takes the face with the largest minimum score (`_spherical_best_face`). On a closed sphere
a point lies in exactly one triangle, the only one with a non-negative score, so a non-negative best *is*
the true container. For large meshes the search is accelerated by a coarse-icosphere spatial index (bin
faces by their centroid's nearest coarse vertex plus its one-ring, search only the query's bucket); any
query whose bucketed best comes out negative - a container that fell outside the searched buckets - falls
back to the exact brute $O(Q\cdot F)$ argmax, so the result is *identical to brute force*, just faster
(`method='auto'` switches on automatically for large $F$). This is clean-room: no `scipy.spatial`.

With the container known, the weights are the *planar barycentric coordinates of the target's radial
projection onto that triangle's plane* (`_spherical_barycentric`): intersect the ray along $t$ with the
plane through $(a,b,c)$, then solve the $2\times2$ barycentric system. The weights are *clipped
non-negative and renormalised* to a partition of unity, so a target that lands fractionally outside every
triangle (a near-degenerate edge case) projects onto the nearest edge/vertex and constants are still
reproduced exactly.

### Two modes, and the conservation property that matters

`surface_resample` returns a *pair* `(operator, resampled)`: the resampling
[`ELL`](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-ell) operator (an $(n_{\text{target}}\times n_{\text{source}})$
sparse map, host-side construction) *and* the field after applying it. Construction is the **HOST-CTOR**
class; the *application* is pure-JAX and differentiable w.r.t. the field, through `apply_operator` over the
[REAL semiring](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-semiring). Reuse the operator for every further field of the
same `(source, target)` pair - you pay the search once. Two methods mirror the two Workbench modes:

- **`method='barycentric'`** - a *forward gather*: each target vertex takes the barycentric blend of the
  source triangle it falls in. The operator is *row-stochastic* (each target row sums to 1), so *constants
  are preserved exactly*. Best for smooth features and up-sampling.
- **`method='adap_bary_area'`** (the default) - *adaptive barycentric, area-weighted*. Here the goal is not
  just smooth interpolation but *conservation of the area-weighted integral*. Each *source* vertex scatters
  its anatomical vertex area into the corners of the target triangle it falls in (a reverse barycentric
  scatter, `_spherical_barycentric(target, source)`), and each target row is normalised by the target
  vertex area. The result conserves
  $$
  \sum_t A^{\text{target}}_t \,\text{out}_t \;=\; \sum_s A^{\text{source}}_s \,\text{in}_s,
  $$ {#eq-conservation}
  exactly, whenever every target vertex receives source mass (the down-sampling / matched regime). Target
  vertices that receive none - up-sampling *holes* - fall back to the forward barycentric gather, where
  conservation becomes approximate. This is the faithful Workbench behaviour and the documented divergence
  between the modes: pick `'barycentric'` when exact constant-preservation matters more than the integral,
  `'adap_bary_area'` when the integral must be conserved.

Why @eq-conservation is the property you want: a parcellation label occupies some fraction of cortical
area, and a scalar like thickness has a meaningful surface mean. Conserving the area-weighted integral
means *label area fractions and area-weighted means survive the resample* - you are not silently inflating
or deflating how much cortex a region covers when you change tessellation. The `*_area` arguments let you
supply the *anatomical* vertex areas (the Workbench `-area-metrics`); they default to the spheres' own
`vertex_areas`, which conserve the *sphere*-area-weighted integral. The conserved inner product is always
w.r.t. the *supplied* areas - if you override them, measure conservation against the same arrays.

```python
op, b_on_a = surface_resample(
    subjB_sphere, thicknessB, subjA_sphere,   # carry B's field onto A's mesh
    method='adap_bary_area',                  # conserve the area-weighted integral
)                                             # op is reusable for B's other maps
```

Up-sampling (a few thousand source vertices onto `fsaverage`'s ~160k) leans on the `barycentric`
smoothness; down-sampling or matched-resolution transfer leans on the `adap_bary_area` conservation. The
same operator handles a multi-channel field (`source_vals` shaped `(n_source, c)`); a 1-D field is squeezed
and restored automatically.

## Metric tools on the sphere {#sec-spherical-conv}

Once your data lives on the sphere, two operations recur: *smoothing* a noisy map while respecting the
surface, and *measuring distance* along it. Both are owned here.

### Great-circle distance: `spherical_geodesic_distance`

The geodesic between two points on a sphere is the *great-circle arc*; its length is $r$ times the angle
$\angle(x,y)$ between the unit vectors. The naive formula $\theta=\arccos(\langle x,y\rangle)$ is
numerically poor for nearly-parallel or nearly-antipodal points, where $\arccos$ has vanishing derivative
and loses precision. The code uses the *`arctan2` form* instead, which is well-conditioned across the whole
range:

$$
d(x,y) \;=\; r\,\operatorname{arctan2}\!\big(\lVert x\times y\rVert,\; \langle x,y\rangle\big).
$$ {#eq-geodesic}

The cross-product magnitude is $\lVert x\rVert\lVert y\rVert\sin\theta\ge0$ and the dot product is
$\lVert x\rVert\lVert y\rVert\cos\theta$, so `arctan2` recovers the unsigned angle in $[0,\pi]$ and handles
the antipodal case via the sign of the denominator - no catastrophic cancellation (`_geodesic_pair` in
source). `spherical_geodesic_distance(X, Y=None, r=1.0)` returns the *all-pairs* matrix $(\dots,n,m)$ (or
pairwise within `X` when `Y is None`), broadcasting leading batch dims. It is pure JAX with no host branch
- jittable and differentiable (**DIFF-JAX**); it is the inner kernel `spherical_conv` calls to build its
neighbour distances, so the two share the exact same numerics. Note the *radius enters only as a
multiplicative scale* - the *angle* is radius-independent, so distances on a radius-100 sphere are just
$100\times$ those on the unit sphere; choose `r` to match the units you report in. (The chapter works
throughout in the Cartesian unit-vector convention the module takes as canonical; if your coordinates
arrive as latitude/longitude - the form @fig-surface-conv uses purely to *display* a sphere flat - convert
once with `latlong_to_cartesian` / its inverse `cartesian_to_latlong` before any of these routines.)

### Spherical Gaussian smoothing: `spherical_conv`

To smooth a field on the sphere - denoise a thickness map, blur an activation before a group test -
convolve it with a Gaussian over *geodesic* distance. The naive implementation forms the full $n\times n$
geodesic matrix, Gaussian-weights it, and reduces: $O(n^2)$ memory and compute, fatal at cortical scale.
The cure is to notice that a Gaussian is *effectively local*: a neighbour many $\sigma$ away contributes
nothing. So restrict each point to its $k$ *nearest neighbours* by geodesic distance, and the operator
becomes sparse - $O(n\cdot k)$. @fig-surface-conv shows the effect: noise on the sphere collapses onto the
underlying smooth field.

Because the kernel depends only on the geodesic distance between points - an $SO(3)$-invariant - the
smoother is *rotation-equivariant*: rotate the sphere and the smoothed field rotates with it, the surface
analogue of the geometry unit's equivariant operators. That is exactly the property a group-level analysis
needs: smoothing commutes with the spherical registration that aligned the subjects, so it does not depend
on how each cortex happens to be oriented on the sphere.

```{python}
#| label: fig-surface-conv
#| fig-cap: "Spherical Gaussian smoothing as a k-NN geodesic-weighted average (icosphere, ~642 vertices, shown unrolled to latitude/longitude). (a) a smooth field corrupted by additive noise; (b) after `spherical_conv` with $\\sigma=0.30$ over each point's 12 nearest neighbours by great-circle distance — the banded structure is recovered. The kernel weights are normalised to sum to 1 per point, so constants are preserved and no energy is injected."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def icosphere(subdiv=3):
    t = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    verts = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=float)
    verts /= np.linalg.norm(verts, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    faces = [
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ]
    for _ in range(subdiv):
        mid = {}; vlist = list(verts); new_faces = []
        def mp(i, j):
            key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
            if key in mid: return mid[key]
            m = vlist[i] + vlist[j]; m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
            vlist.append(m); mid[key] = len(vlist) - 1; return mid[key]
        for (a, b, c) in faces:
            ab, bc, ca = mp(a, b), mp(b, c), mp(c, a)
            new_faces += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
        verts = np.array(vlist); faces = new_faces
    return np.array(verts)

V = icosphere(3); n = V.shape[0]

def geodesic(X, Y):
    cross = np.linalg.norm(np.cross(X[:, None, :], Y[None, :, :]), axis=-1)
    return np.arctan2(cross, X @ Y.T)

D = geodesic(V, V)
k = 12
knn = np.argsort(D, axis=1)[:, :k]

clean = np.cos(3 * V[:, 2]) + 0.5 * np.sin(4 * V[:, 0])
noisy = clean + rng.normal(scale=0.6, size=n)

sigma = 0.30
sm = np.empty(n)
for i in range(n):
    nb = knn[i]
    w = np.exp(-0.5 * (D[i, nb] / sigma) ** 2); w /= w.sum()
    sm[i] = w @ noisy[nb]

lat = np.arcsin(np.clip(V[:, 2], -1, 1)); lon = np.arctan2(V[:, 1], V[:, 0])
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))
vmin, vmax = clean.min() - 0.5, clean.max() + 0.5
for ax, data, title in zip(axes, (noisy, sm),
                           ("(a) noisy field on $S^2$",
                            "(b) after spherical Gaussian conv\n(k-NN geodesic weights, $\\sigma=0.30$)")):
    sc = ax.scatter(lon, lat, c=data, cmap="magma", s=22, vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
    ax.set_title(title); ax.set_xlabel("longitude"); ax.set_ylabel("latitude")
    fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, shrink=0.8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The real value of `spherical_conv` is that it is *not* a bespoke loop: it *specialises onto the geometry
unit's sparse substrate*. The k-NN indices form a per-point neighbour list - an
[ELL adjacency](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-ell), the $(n,k)$ index/value layout - and the reduction is
one call to `semiring_ell_matmul` over the [REAL semiring](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-semiring):

$$
\text{out}_{i,c} \;=\; \sum_{p=1}^{k} W_{i,p}\,\text{data}_{\,\text{idx}_{i,p},\,c},
\qquad
W_{i,p} \;=\; \frac{\exp\!\big(-\tfrac12 (d_{i,p}/\sigma)^2\big)}{\sum_{q} \exp\!\big(-\tfrac12 (d_{i,q}/\sigma)^2\big)}.
$$ {#eq-sphconv}

Here $d_{i,p}$ is the geodesic distance (@eq-geodesic) from point $i$ to its $p$-th neighbour, $W$ are the
row-normalised Gaussian weights, and the sum is the semiring matmul. The *row-normalisation is
load-bearing*: each row sums to 1, so a constant field is preserved and the smoother injects no energy. The
call is

```python
out = spherical_conv(
    data, coor,                  # data (..., n, c); coor (n, 3) Cartesian
    sigma=...,                   # kernel width, in the units of r (radians * r)
    neighbourhood=k,             # int k -> on-the-fly k-NN (O(n^2) memory)
    #             | (n, k)       # OR a precomputed index array (large meshes)
    r=1.0,
    truncate=None,               # optional hard cutoff: neighbours beyond -> weight 0
)
```

Two practical knobs. First, `neighbourhood`: passing an `int` computes the k-NN on the fly, which is
$O(n^2)$ *memory* (the row-by-row `lax.map` keeps peak memory $O(n)$ but compute is still $O(n^2)$) - fine
for an icosphere, but for a real cortical mesh ($n\gtrsim10^4$) you should *precompute the adjacency* (e.g.
the icosphere's natural $k$-ring) and pass the explicit `(n, k)` index array, dropping the construction to
$O(n\cdot k)$. Second, `truncate` sets a hard geodesic cutoff so a $k$-NN that happens to reach a far point
gives it zero weight. Multi-channel data is smoothed depthwise - the same spatial kernel per channel; for a
per-channel $\sigma$, call repeatedly. The whole reduction is jittable and differentiable (DIFF-JAX) once
the adjacency is in hand.

## Connecting outward {#sec-connections}

**Averaging spherical maps - building a surface template.** A surface template like `fsaverage` is, in
spirit, an *average* of many subjects' spherical maps. Averaging maps is the same Riemannian-manifold
problem the linear rungs already solved: you cannot take an entrywise mean of points on a curved space and
expect to land back on it. The [transform manifold](transforms.qmd#sec-transform-manifold) page gives the
general recipe - the Karcher / Fréchet mean via `log`/`exp` on the manifold - and the same idea governs
averaging on $(S^2)^n$: iterate a tangent-space mean and retract, exactly the geometry this chapter's
`_retract` already uses. Building and refining a surface template (the `josa`/`sugar` registration lineage)
composes `spherical_parameterize` to put each subject on the sphere, a spherical registration to align
anatomy, and a manifold mean to fuse them.

**Forward to parcellation transfer.** The headline consumer of `surface_resample` - moving an atlas
*parcellation* (a per-vertex label field) from a template onto a subject, or pooling a connectivity feature
within parcels - lives in a future *connectivity / labels* unit and is out of scope here. What this chapter
guarantees is the substrate that unit will stand on: `adap_bary_area` conservation (@eq-conservation) is
precisely what keeps a label's area fraction honest under resampling, and the reusable `ELL` operator is
what makes transferring a whole atlas (many label channels, one search) cheap.

## Further reading

- Fischl, B., Sereno, M. I., and Dale, A. M. (1999). "Cortical surface-based analysis. II: Inflation,
  flattening, and a surface-based coordinate system." *NeuroImage*, 9(2), 195–207. FreeSurfer's
  `mris_sphere`: the metric/area-distortion relaxation onto the sphere this chapter's iterative refinement
  follows.
- Henschel, L., Conjeti, S., Estrada, S., Diers, K., Fischl, B., and Reuter, M. (2020). "FastSurfer — A
  fast and accurate deep learning based neuroimaging pipeline." *NeuroImage*, 219, 117012. The spectral
  one-shot embedding (`recon_surf/spherically_project.py`): the first three non-constant Laplace–Beltrami
  eigenfunctions as a spherical map, with a flipped-area quality gate.
- Gu, X., Wang, Y., Chan, T. F., Thompson, P. M., and Yau, S.-T. (2004). "Genus zero surface conformal
  mapping and its application to brain surface mapping." *IEEE TMI*, 23(8), 949–958. The discrete
  uniformisation view: a genus-0 cortex is conformally a sphere.
- Choi, P. T., Lam, K. C., and Lui, L. M. (2015). "FLASH: Fast landmark aligned spherical harmonic
  parameterization for genus-0 closed brain surfaces." *SIAM J. Imaging Sciences*, 8(1), 67–94.
  Conformal-energy minimisation: why $E_{\text{conf}}$ collapses and how the area term + Möbius
  normalisation prevent it (the basis for @eq-area).
- Robinson, E. C., et al. (2014). "MSM: A new flexible framework for multimodal surface matching."
  *NeuroImage*, 100, 414–426. Multimodal surface matching on the sphere (`MSMSulc`, `MSMAll`); the
  registration that aligns two parameterised spheres before resampling.
- Glasser, M. F., et al. (2013). "The minimal preprocessing pipelines for the Human Connectome Project."
  *NeuroImage*, 80, 105–124. The `fs_LR`/`fsaverage` and Connectome Workbench `-metric-resample`
  (`ADAP_BARY_AREA`) ecosystem that `surface_resample` mirrors.
- Tutte, W. T. (1963). "How to draw a graph." *Proc. London Math. Soc.*, 3(1), 743–767. The fold-free
  barycentric-embedding guarantee underlying the bijective-init theory.

## See also

- The spherical primitives in this chapter —
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/sphere.py)
  (`spherical_parameterize`, `surface_resample`, `spherical_conv`, `spherical_geodesic_distance`, and the
  spectral / bijectivity helpers).
- The sparse-operator substrate the conv and resample stand on —
  [`src/nitrix/sparse/`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse) (`ELL`,
  `apply_operator`, `vertex_areas`, `face_areas`, `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`, `icosphere`) and
  [`src/nitrix/semiring/`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/semiring)
  (`semiring_ell_matmul`, `REAL`).
- Geometry unit — the spectral embedding, signed spherical areas and bijectivity test this chapter builds
  on ([spectral embedding](../geometry/spectral.qmd#sec-embedding)); the cotangent Laplace–Beltrami
  operator behind @eq-conformal ([Laplace–Beltrami](../geometry/laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent)); the
  ELL / semiring substrate ([operators](../geometry/operators.qmd#sec-ell)); and the execution-class
  taxonomy ([foundations](../geometry/foundations.qmd#sec-classes)).
- Neighbouring registration pages: the [transform ladder](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder) this chapter tops
  out; the [transform manifold](transforms.qmd#sec-transform-manifold) for averaging spherical maps into a
  template; and the dense-field rung below this one ([Diffeomorphic](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-svf)).
